ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for Satay Kambing; A0FKP (grilled meat) used as closest defensible parent anchor with mutton/goat qualifier noted.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for satay kambing; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 5 skewers with peanut sauce and accompaniments)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Satay Kambing is approximately 250 g, comprising around 5 mutton skewers served with peanut dipping sauce, ketupat, cucumber slices, and raw onion.

nutrient_source.title: IMR Malaysia MyFCD — Satay (Mutton/Goat, Grilled) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, cooked (NDB 17083) used as cross-reference for micronutrient values
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macro values anchored to IMR Malaysia MyFCD satay (mutton) entry. Micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lamb composite (NDB 17083) and adjusted for marinade and peanut sauce contribution. Saturated fat estimated from USDA cooked lamb data. Input enriched_json values are consistent with these approved sources and have been retained where corroborated.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate: mixed meat dish with marinade, GI ~40 (low); University of Sydney GI Database entry for satay/grilled meat with peanut sauce used as closest analogous food.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Satay Kambing found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 (low) estimated from the closest analogous food: grilled marinated meat with peanut sauce, which is high in protein and fat, slowing glucose absorption. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 18.0) / 100 = 7.2. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg per 100 g) from marinade and peanut sauce; saturated fat from mutton
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 2–3 skewers, request reduced-sodium peanut sauce, and avoid adding extra salt, as a full 250 g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium — nearly 40% of the recommended daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) due to high protein and fat content, but sugar in marinade and peanut sauce requires monitoring
type2_diabetes.guidance: Satay Kambing has a low GI (~40) and moderate carbohydrate load (GL ~7.2 per serving), making it a relatively suitable protein-rich option for people with Type 2 Diabetes, provided ketupat portions are minimised and peanut sauce is used sparingly to limit added sugar intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from mutton (~5.8 g per 100 g) and sodium from marinade and peanut sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of Satay Kambing due to its saturated fat and sodium content; choosing leaner cuts, limiting peanut sauce, and pairing with vegetables rather than ketupat can reduce the overall cardiovascular risk profile of the meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, galangal) may be beneficial, but red meat and charcoal-grilled foods may be triggers in some individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The turmeric and galangal in the marinade contain anti-inflammatory compounds that may benefit rheumatoid arthritis patients; however, individuals who find red meat or grilled foods to be personal triggers should limit intake and monitor symptoms.

peanut_allergy.key_factor: Peanut sauce (kuah kacang) is a primary accompaniment and a major allergen
peanut_allergy.guidance: Satay Kambing is contraindicated for individuals with peanut allergy due to the peanut dipping sauce; the dish should be avoided entirely or the sauce must be completely omitted and cross-contamination risk assessed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Satay Kambing is a traditional Malay grilled meat dish consisting of bite-sized pieces of mutton (goat meat) that are marinated in a fragrant blend of lemongrass, turmeric, galangal, coriander seeds, cumin, and palm sugar, then threaded onto bamboo skewers and grilled over a charcoal fire. The charcoal grilling imparts a distinctive smoky aroma and caramelised crust to the meat, while the marinade tenderises the mutton and delivers a deep, savory-sweet flavour profile. It is traditionally served with kuah kacang (spicy peanut dipping sauce), ketupat (compressed rice cakes), fresh cucumber slices, and raw onion wedges. Nutritionally, Satay Kambing is a high-protein, moderate-fat food providing approximately 18.2 g protein and 15.5 g fat per 100 g, with a relatively low carbohydrate content of 7.2 g per 100 g. It is a good source of zinc (5.2 mg), vitamin B12 (2.2 mcg), niacin (4.8 mg), and selenium (22.5 mcg). The sodium content is notable at 380 mg per 100 g, largely from the marinade and peanut sauce. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), making it a relatively blood-glucose-friendly protein source when ketupat is limited. Clinically, it is suitable in moderation for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and high protein content, but sodium and saturated fat levels warrant caution for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is a staple of Malay street food culture, commonly found at hawker centres and pasar malam (night markets) across Malaysia and Singapore, and is popular as a snack, appetiser, or main course at lunch and dinner.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mutton satay?
Is satay kambing suitable for diabetics?
What is the glycemic index of satay?
What nutrients does mutton satay contain?
Can I eat satay kambing if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between satay kambing and satay ayam?
meal_context: high-protein main course at lunch or dinner

confidence_reason: Primary macro values are consistent with IMR Malaysia MyFCD satay (mutton) entries and corroborated by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lamb data. Micronutrient values are cross-referenced with USDA approved sources. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and water values are estimated from USDA cooked lamb composite data rather than a direct Satay Kambing entry, warranting medium rather than high confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework.
notes: Saturated fat (5.8 g), monounsaturated fat (5.2 g), polyunsaturated fat (2.1 g), trans fat (0.1 g), cholesterol (72.0 mg), and water (55.0 g) per 100 g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lamb composite (NDB 17083) as no direct fatty acid profile for Satay Kambing with peanut sauce is available in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. All core macros (protein_g 18.2, fat_g 15.5, carbohydrate_g 7.2) are non-zero and nutritionally consistent with a grilled marinated mutton dish. No zero-macro justification is required. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (40 × 18.0 g carbohydrate per 250 g serving) / 100 = 7.2. The input source 'Malaysian Journal of Medical Sciences' is not on the approved source list and has been replaced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central as primary approved sources. FoodEx2 code A0FKP used as closest parent anchor for grilled meat; no dedicated Satay Kambing code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026-04-16. foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' per allowed values in VERITAS_SCORING_MODEL_v2.md.
